Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## AN ATTENTION FREE TRANSFORMER

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

We introduce Attention Free Transformer (AFT), an efficient variant of Transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017) that eliminates the need for dot product attention. AFT offers great simplicity and efficiency compared with standard Transformers, where the multi-head attention operation is replaced with the composition of element-wise multiplications/divisions and global/local pooling. During training time, AFT has linear time and space complexity w.r.t. both the sequence length and feature dimension; in the autoregressive decoding mode, AFT has constant memory and time complexity per step. We show that, surprisingly, we are able to train AFT effectively on challenging benchmarks, and also to match or surpass the standard Transformer counterparts and other efficient variants. In particular, AFT achieves the state-of-the-art result on CIFAR10 autoregressive modeling with much reduced complexity, and also outperforms several efficient Transformer variants on Enwik8.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Attention mechanisms, represented by Transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017), have driven the advancement of various machine learning problems, including language modeling (Devlin et al., 2018; Radford et al.), image modeling (Chen et al.), and set modeling (Lee et al., 2019). Different from other well known model architectures such as Convolutional Neural Nets (CNNs) or Recurrent Neural Nets (RNNs), Transformers enable direct interaction between every pair of elements within a sequence, which makes them especially powerful at capturing long term dependencies.

However, Transformers require high computational costs. The root cause of this challenge is the need to perform attention operations that have quadratic time and space complexity w.r.t the context size. This makes it especially difficult for Transformers to scale to inputs with large context sizes. A number of recent works have been dedicated to addressing the scalability issue of Transformers (Child et al., 2019; Kitaev et al., 2020; Rae et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020b; Katharopoulos et al., 2020; Tay et al., 2020a; Choromanski et al., 2020). While the techniques adopted in the literature range from sparsity, locality sensitive hashing, low rank decomposition, kernel approximation and etc., most of them are trying to approximate the full attention operation.

In this paper, we take a bolder step towards the same goal, by proposing a computational module that does not use or approximate the standard dot product attention. We hence name our model the attention free transformer (AFT). Similar to dot product attention, AFT is composed of the interaction of three quantities, namely the query, key and value. What’s different, however, is that AFT operates solely based on element-wise operations. To be more concrete, they key and value are first multiplied element-wise, the result of which is then pooled over the context dimension (in the causal model, this corresponds to a cumulative sum). The query is then multiplied with the reduced key-value representation element-wise to produce the final output. See Figure 1a for an illustration.

AFT maintains the full advantage of dot product attention, namely direct interaction between any two elements in a sequence (up to proper masking). However, the computational cost is drastically reduced to a _O_ ( _Td_ ) complexity for time and space, where _T, d_ are the context length and feature dimension, respectively. In the autoregressive decoding mode, AFT also provides constant decoding time and space complexity per step, compared to _O_ ( _T_ ) for standard transformers. To the best of our knowledge, AFT is the first model that achieves such efficiency in the context of Transformers. See Table 1 for the complexity analysis of AFT in comparison to other variants.

We show that we can interpret AFT as an extreme case of multi head dot product attention (MHA). In particular, we show that by 1) setting the number of heads equal to the feature dimension in MHA and 2) using _relu_ in place of _softmax_ as the non-linearity, MHA can be decomposed into the summation of two AFT modules (see Equation 6). However, this relationship is not true in a general sense, i.e., by varying the non-linearity injected after the query and key in AFT, we can obtain models that do not have a MHA counterpart. This realization allows us to freely explore the design choices (e.g., nonlinearity) of AFT to achieve the best performance. This philosophy is in direct contrast with previous and concurrent “linearized attention” works (Katharopoulos et al., 2020; Choromanski et al., 2020), which are constrained by the design space of MHA.

We perform experiments with AFT on several benchmarks, including unconditional image modeling, image super-resolution, language modeling, machine translation and point cloud generation. We show that AFT works very well as an alternative to the standard Transformer, providing competitive results as well as excellent efficiency.

To summarize, our contributions are as follows:

- We propose AFT, a new family of Transformer models that achieves _O_ ( _Td_ ) time and space complexity in training, as well as _O_ ( _d_ ) time and space complexity in autoregressive decoding.

- We show strong performance of AFT as a drop in replacement of MHA on various benchmarks, including setting the state-of-the-art result on CIFAR10 in the standard setting and outperforming other efficient Transformer variants.

## 2 MULTI-HEAD ATTENTION

At the core of Transformers is the Multi-Head Attention (MHA) operation. Given three sequences, namely the query _Q ∈ R[T][ ×][d]_ , key _K ∈ R[T][ ×][d]_ and value _V ∈ R[T][ ×][d]_ , and the number of heads _h_ , MHA performs a scaled dot product attention for each head _i_ , defined as:

where _Wi[Q] ∈ R[d][×][d][k]_ , _Wi[K] ∈ R[d][×][d][k]_ , _Wi[V] ∈ R[d][×][d][v]_ are linear transformations for head _i_ , and _σ_ is the non-linearity by default set as the _softmaxr_ function (subscript _r_ indicates softmax is applied to each row of a matrix). _dk, dv_ are dimensions for key and value, respectively. MHA concatenates the output of _h_ attention heads along the channel dimension, resulting in feature dimension _hdv_ . Unless otherwise mentioned, we assume _dk_ = _dv_ and _h_ = _ddk_[.][This means the query, key and value] are the same dimension within each head, and output dimension matches that of the input.

## 3 METHODOLOGY

## 3.1 ATTENTION FREE TRANSFORMER

We now define Attention free transformer (AFT), which provides an alternative to MHA. Given _Q, K, V_ , AFT first linearly transforms them into _Q[′]_ = _QW[Q]_ , _K[′]_ = _KW[K]_ , _V[′]_ = _V W[V]_ , then

where _⊙_ is the element-wise product, with support for broadcasting when the operands’ dimensions don’t exactly match[1] ; _σq, σk_ are nonlinearities applied to the query and key, respectively. Explained in words, the key and value are first combined with an element-wise multiplication, the result of which is then pooled over the context dimension, yielding a fixed length context vector _∈ R[d]_ . This context vector is then multiplied with each row of the query, which forms the final output of an AFT layer.

One particularly useful variant of MHA is masked attention, oftentimes presented in the form of causal attention. Specifically, in auto-regressive models, queries are constrained to not be able to interact with keys and values beyond the curret position. In standard attention, this is usually implemented with an explicit binary masking matrix of shape _T ×T_ , with non-causal entries masked as 0. We show that it is also straightforward to extend AFT to the causal mode while maintaining its efficiency. We denote an AFT layer’s output as _Yt_ = _f_ ( _Q≤t, K≤t, V≤t_ ) _, t_ = 1 _, ..., T_[2] . **Discussions:** The design philosophy of AFT is to promote extreme efficiency, while keeping the benefit of standard Transformers. Concretely, AFT enables _direct_ interaction of any two elements within the sequence, which is arguably the biggest advantage of Transformers over other types of models such as RNNs and ConvNets. However, AFT gets rid of the need of performing the costly spatial dot product attention, by computing a reduced value representation with the weights only depending on the keys. The resulting operation has an extremely efficiency of _O_ ( _Td_ ) w.r.t. both time and space, which is the first model that achieves **linear complexity along both context and feature dimensions** . Moreover, the causal mode of AFT has an additional advantage of a constant decoding cost per step, similar to (Katharopoulos et al., 2020). To see this, from Equation 3, we have a simple recursion of _Yt_ = _σq_ ( _Q[′] t_[)] _[⊙]_[(] _[σ][k]_[(] _[K] t[′]_[)] _[⊙][V] t[′]_[+] _[KV][t][−]_[1][)][ with] _[ KV][t]_[=][ �] _[t] t[′]_ =1 � _σk_ ( _Kt[′][′]_[)] _[⊙][V] t[′][′]_ �, assuming _σq, σk_ are both element-wise functions. One thus only need to keep _KVt_ in memory, and update it with constant cost per step.

**Selecting nonlinearies:** _σq, σk_ provide additional nonlinearity which helps to increase model’s capacity. Empirically, we have found that one particularly strong setting is to let _σk_ = _softmax_ which is normalized along the context dimension. This choice brings an interesting benefit especially in the causal mode, which we can explicitly write as:

Here _gt_ ( _t_ ) acts as a role similar to that of an input gate in an LSTM, and _gt_ ( _t[′]_ ) is operating like the forget gate, which depends on the input of time _t_ , dynamically downweights the contribution of past time steps. When augmented with standard position embeddings as commonly used in Transformers, this allows the model to be able to learn the notion of recency at the same time of having access to the full context in the history. From this view, _σq_ can also be interpreted as the output gate, for which we found that both _sigmoid_ and _relu_ work well, with the former being slightly better. Also note that the same space and time complexity still holds for _σk_ = _softmax_ , both in training and decoding. In our experiments, unless otherwise mentioned, we use the _sigmoid_ + _softmax_ setting for _σq_ and _σk_ by default.

## 4 RELATED WORK

Since the Transformer was introduced, there have been numerous attempts to address the major source of inefficiency in the architecture, the quadratic cost of the attention operation. Improving this

**Approximating the dot product** . Katharopoulos et al. (2020); Choromanski et al. (2020) both propose to approximate the exponential kernel with inner product of projections, which leads to a linearized attention operation of complexity _O_ ( _Td_[2] ). AFT is similar but offers greater efficiency of _O_ ( _Td_ ) due to the exclusive use of element-wise operations, as well as more design flexibility. Reformers (Kitaev et al., 2020) apply LSH as an approximation to the dot product, whereas AFT completely gets rid the need of dot product.

**Sparse, local attention** . Sparse Transformers (Child et al., 2019) and Image Transformer (Parmar et al., 2018) proposes to use fixed sparse or local context patterns. Attention models in vision tasks (often combined with convolutions) use image structure to help handcraft relevant spatial patterns to attend (Wang et al., 2020a; Huang et al., 2019b; Zhu et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2019a; Ramachandran et al., 2019). AFT also borrows the locality idea, but we put it as a bias rather than hard constraint (see AFT-local-learned) . Also AFT is a standalone module, where it works as a plug in replacement of MHA in autoregressive tasks.

**Context compression** . Other approaches try to learn context patterns. Adaptive-Span Transformers (Sukhbaatar et al., 2019) learn a range for each attention head within which to attend. Routing transformers (Roy et al., 2020) use clustering to compute dot-product attention only over a subset of elements within the same cluster. The Linformer (Wang et al., 2020b) reduces the length of the context by compressing the keys and values with a linear layer. Compressive Transformers (Rae et al., 2020) compute and update reduced representations of the input that are far enough back in the input sequence, and attend to those compressed representations. AFT is largely complementary to these approaches, as our focus is to improve the complexity of any given sequence from the operation level.

**Eliminating dot product attention** . Instead of limiting the number of comparisons, other methods change the operation used to compute attention. The Synthesizer (Tay et al., 2020a) uses attention weights predicted from inputs, rather than derived from dot-product interactions. The LightConv module introduced in (Wu et al., 2019) proposes to replace the dot product self-attention with dynamic lightweight depthwise convolution, where the weights are normalized across temporal dimension. The Sinkhorn Transformer (Tay et al., 2020b) uses a differentiable sorting operation to identify relevant comparisons that may not be local in the original sequence order. AFT can be viewed as a more drastic version in this direction, where the we adopt a single global ”attention mask” ( _w_ ) of all ones (vanilla AFT) or with a few learnable entries (AFT-local-learned).

**Gated RNNs** . AFT is also related to the classic line of work on gated RNN variants, including LSTMs Hochreiter & Schmidhuber (1997), GRUs (Chung et al., 2014) and QuasiRNNs (Bradbury et al., 2016). AFT maintains the benefit of RNN models (linear complexity w.r.t. sequence length, constant decoding cost), but offers great parallelism and effectiveness, thanks to the use of a simple context reduction operation, which is amendable to a fully parallel implementation during training. We believe that AFT also offers new perspectives for rethinking the success and limitations of gated RNNs.

**Dynamic Convolution** . AFT is also related to dynamic convolution (Wu et al., 2019) when applied to auto-regressive tasks, where the reduced key-value representation can be interpreted as a per sequence convolutional kernel. However, AFT operates in an extreme case where the dimension of the kernel is 1 along both the feature and spatial dimensions, again presenting superior efficiency.

## 5 EXPERIMENTS

We conduct experiments on five tasks: unconditional image modeling (Sec. 5.2), language modeling (Sec. 5.3), machine translation (Sec. 5.4), image super resolution (Sec. B.2) and point cloud generation (Sec. B.3). We focus on the causal mode of AFT, while leaving systematic evaluation of the non-causal version as future work. Unless otherwise mentioned, all experiments are conducted on 8 _×_ V100 GPU machines.

## 5.1 EFFICIENCY

To support our analysis in Table 1, we benchmarked an implementation of AFT on a single forward and backward pass on random data with a batch size of 4. We compared AFT with the self-attention from Transformers, a linear attention mechanism from (Katharopoulos et al., 2020), and a Reformer (Kitaev et al., 2020). For all of these, we used a base architecture with 12 layers, 8 heads (except for AFT, where there are no heads), feature dimension of either 256 or 1024, and context lengths up to 10,000. We used the code from the fast transformers library to perform the evaluations[3] . Results in terms of runtime and peak GPU usage are in Figure 2. Where data-points do not exist in the figure, the model exhausted GPU memory. We see from this that compared to Transformers and Reformers, AFT and linear attention require far fewer computational resources as context increases. In addition, we see that AFT is not as sensitive to the feature dimension as the linear attention, which expands the design space for feasible models. In all settings, AFT performs best. ## 5.2 UNCONDITIONAL IMAGE MODELING

In our first set of experiments, we consider the problem of image modeling by minimizing the negative log likelihood (NLL). Similar to Parmar et al. (2018), we represent an RGB image as a sequence of length _H × W ×_ 3, with _H, W_ being the height and width, respectively. Each sub-pixel is represented as a 256-way discrete variable. We use CIFAR10 for image density modeling.

**Feasibility study and choice of nonlinearities.** We first conduct experiments validating the legitimacy of AFT and its four nonlinearity variants. Our reference Transformer design largely follows that of Chen et al., where a transformer block consists of an attention layer (AFT layer in our cae) with residual connection and a 2 layer MLP with residual connections. Layer Normalization (LN) (Ba et al., 2016) is applied in a “pre-act” fashion. We adopt learned position embeddings, and use a set of shared token embeddings and prediction heads across RGB.

Our base architecture consists of 24 Transformer blocks, each with d=256 dimensional features. The hidden layer of the MLP per block has 4 dimensionality of its input. We use Adam, and follow a standard warmup learning rate schedule as in Vaswani et al. (2017). We use an initial learning rate of 3 _×_ 10 _[−]_[3] and a weight decay of 0 _._ 1 applied to all linear transformations weights, and a dropout of 0.1.

We adopt simple data augmentation. During training, we first randomly flip each image horizontally, then add or subtract a value in the range [ _−_ 10 _,_ 10] from all its subpixels, and clip resulting pixel values to [0 _,_ 255]. We use cross entropy loss, and a default batch size of 128 for 200 training epochs. We train three versions of AFT, namely AFT-relu2 (Equation 6), AFT-relu ( _σq_ = _σk_ = _relu_ ), AFT-relu-softmax: ( _σq_ = _relu_ , _σk_ = _softmax_ ), AFT-sigmoid-softmax ( _σq_ = _sigmoid_ , _σk_ = _softmax_ ), all of which use full contexts. We show the training and test loss curves in Figure 4. All versions of AFT are trainable with standard optimization techniques for Transformers. In particular, AFT-relu2 performs slightly worse than AFT-relu, and both are significantly worse than AFT-relu-softmax and AFT-sigmoid-softmax. AFT-sigmoid-softmax also outperforms the strong PixelCNN++ baseline (Salimans et al., 2017). Based on this observation, we use AFT-sigmoidsoftmax as the default setting for all remaining experiments, unless otherwise mentioned.
**Comparing with the state of the art.** CIFAR10 is a crowded benchmark for image autoregressive modeling, and we compare with a few competitive baselines, as shown in Table 2. Note that

AFT achieve the state-of-the-art result in this setting, with much fewer parameters, better speed and significantly less memory.

CIFAR10 has an unrolled sequence length of 3072, which is already prohibiting to train a full Transformer with reasonable size. For example, for a standard 12 layer 512 dimension and 8 head configuration, the maximum batch size we can fit in our 8 V100 node is only 16, which makes it infeasible already. Our closest baseline Image Transformer (Parmar et al., 2018), which restricts attention to local2d windows of size of 256. We test our AFT-local-learned and aft-local2d-hard variants, with the same window size, and the same architecture as well as a deeper but narrower one (24 layer and 256 dimensions), which are still fair comparisons. We also compare to Sparse Transformers (Child et al., 2019), which restrains attention to sparse but global subset of context elements.

From Table2, we see that all AFT variants outperform the Image Transformer baseline. Both AFT local versions are better than the full counterpart, with AFT-local-learned being significantly stronger than others. We also observe that the deeper but narrower architecture is more effective than the shallow but wide baseline. Our best model also achieves the state-of-the-art result on CIFAR10 in this setting, outperforming a much larger Sparse Transformer model. Efficiency wise, we benchmarked Image Transformer against AFT variants on a 8 V100 GPU node[4] . All our variants are faster than Image Transformer, while consuming only half of the memory[5] .

## 5.3 LANGUAGE MODELING

We apply AFT to character level language modeling on Enwik8 (Mahoney, 2011), which is another popular benchmark for auto-regressive modeling. We follow the standard preprocessing procedures and training/validation/test splits as in (Dai et al., 2019). Our base Transformer reference is a 12 layer 512 dimensional 8 head architecture with 2048 feed forward dimensions. For the first set of experiments, we use sequence length of 1024. Our training protocol as largely the same as before, except that we increase the weight decay to 0.5 and train for 100 epochs with batch size 128 in all experiments. We evaluate the AFT-local-learned variant with a window size of 32 and _du_ = 256. We also compare to several efficient Transformer baselines, namely Reformer (Kitaev et al., 2020), Synthesizer (Tay et al., 2020a) and Linear Transformer (Katharopoulos et al., 2020). From Table 3, we see that with the base _L_ = 12 _, d_ = 512 architecture, AFT achieves the lowest training bits per character (bpc), indicating its high capacity. Its test performance is slightly worse than that of the basic Transformer, but outperforms all other three variants. The deeper and narrower architecture of AFT strikes the best balance across parameter, speed, memory and performance. Its test bpc is only 0.02 away from the full Transformer’s, while only consuming a third of the memory and provides a 44% speedup. In the end, we have also trained the same architecture with a sequence length of 2048, which results in an improved performance both on the training and test set. This suggests AFT’s ability to effectively model long range dependencies.

## 5.4 MACHINE TRANSLATION

As a machine translation benchmark, we show experiments with the WMT 2014 English to German translation task. The training set contains approximately 4.5 million sentence pairs. We compare against a Transformer architecture baseline using the OpenNMT implementation (Klein et al., 2017). For translation, the standard architecture is an encoder-decoder structure, where the encoder uses non-causal attention to encode the input sentence. The decoder uses two different types of attention. The first, self attention, sequentially attends to the output translation as it is being generated token by token. The second attends to the translation and the context from the encoder.

In our experiments, we replace the multi-headed decoder self-attention blocks. We compare perplexity (PPL), BLEU score, and efficiency between the Transformer base and AFT in Table 5. In this task, we see that AFT performs on par with the Transformer. As expected for the small context size, typically around 50 tokens, AFT does not show dramatic improvements in speed or memory.

Table 5: WMT 2014 English-to-German Translation.

|Method|TrainingPPL|Validation PPL|Test BLEU score|tokens/sec|GB/GPU|
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
|Transformer|4.38|4.06|27.32|54.7K|7.80|
|AFT(Ours)|4.34|4.10|27.70|54.4K|7.54|

## 6 CONCLUSIONS

We have introduced the Attention Free Transformer that replaces attention with an efficient, easyto-implement new operation. We have demonstrated strong results on challenging benchmarks, despite of the simplicity of our design. We believe that our model opens a new design space for Transformer-like models, and will see impact in various areas where Transformers are applied.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: This paper proposes an interesting and potentially impactful contribution: an "Attention Free Transformer" (AFT) that replaces dot-product attention with element-wise operations and pooling, achieving O(Td) training complexity and O(d) per-step decoding complexity. The claimed empirical results—state-of-the-art on CIFAR10 autoregressive modeling and competitive performance on Enwik8—would be significant if robust, and the breadth of evaluation (image modeling, super-resolution, language modeling, machine translation, point clouds) is commendable. However, several concerns are already apparent from the introduction and early sections. First, the central conceptual claim that AFT is "attention free" is questionable, since the proposed operation still computes a query-modulated weighted combination of value representations via element-wise gating—arguably a form of attention itself; the paper should more carefully position itself relative to concurrent linearized attention work (Katharopoulos et al., Performer) rather than merely asserting its design space is broader. Second, the derivation presenting AFT as an "extreme case" of MHA with heads=d and ReLU substitution feels post-hoc and would benefit from clearer motivation about why this particular factorization should work well. Third, the complexity table understates an important point: while training is O(Td), the memory savings over efficient baselines like Linear Transformer (O(Td + d²)) are modest for typical d, so the practical advantage hinges strongly on empirical quality—making rigorous, apples-to-apples comparisons with identical parameter counts, training budgets, and hyperparameters essential. Clarity is generally good, though the notation around broadcasting and the pooling operation could be tightened, and Figure 1a is referenced before the mechanism is fully specified. Overall, the work is promising but needs sharper conceptual framing, more careful comparison with closely related linearized-attention methods, and thorough ablations to substantiate its claims.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper presents a potentially interesting and timely simplification of Transformer attention, with a clear motivation around reducing the quadratic cost of dot-product attention and an appealingly simple proposed mechanism with linear training complexity and constant-time autoregressive decoding. The high-level exposition is generally understandable, and the claimed empirical breadth—image modeling, language modeling, translation, and point clouds—suggests the authors aim to establish AFT as a serious drop-in alternative rather than a narrow efficiency trick. However, the central conceptual claims need much more careful qualification: the assertion that AFT “maintains the full advantage” of attention via direct interaction between all sequence elements seems overstated, since the described key-value pooling appears to collapse token-specific pairwise interactions into a global or cumulative summary modulated elementwise by the query, which is likely less expressive than standard attention unless positional/local biases or other mechanisms compensate. The novelty should also be more clearly situated relative to linear attention, pooling-based sequence models, gated global-context modules, and related efficient Transformer variants; simply not approximating softmax attention does not by itself establish a fundamentally new design space. The complexity discussion is attractive but should include practical wall-clock/memory measurements, constant factors, implementation details, and decoding-cache assumptions, especially for fair comparison with optimized attention variants. Clarity is weakened by notation and formatting issues in the excerpt, including missing equations, minor typos, ambiguous broadcasting conventions, and an underdeveloped explanation of the claimed relationship between AFT and extreme multi-head attention. Overall, the paper has a promising and elegant idea, but its most significant risks are overclaiming expressivity and “attention-free” advantages, insufficient theoretical characterization of what dependencies AFT can or cannot model, and the need for especially rigorous empirical comparisons and ablations to show that performance gains are due to the proposed mechanism rather than architectural or tuning differences.

comments.title: Complexity claims appear to exclude the dense Q/K/V projections
comments.quote: The resulting operation has an extremely efficiency of _O_ ( _Td_ ) w.r.t. both time and space, which is the first model that achieves **linear complexity along both context and feature dimensions** . Moreover, the causal mode of AFT has an additional advantage of a constant decoding cost per step, similar to (Katharopoulos et al., 2020).
comments.explanation: The attention-free mixing part of AFT, after Q/K/V have already been projected, can indeed be O(Td), and the causal update can be O(d) per step with cached state. However, AFT is defined as first applying dense projections Q′ = QW^Q, K′ = KW^K, V′ = VW^V. Those projections cost O(Td^2) during training and O(d^2) per autoregressive step if counted as part of the layer. Standard attention-complexity comparisons often omit shared projection costs, but the wording says the model/layer achieves linear complexity in both context and feature dimensions and constant decoding cost. This should be qualified as applying to the AFT mixing operation excluding dense input/output projections.

comments.title: Claim that AFT preserves the full pairwise-interaction advantage of attention is overstated
comments.quote: AFT maintains the full advantage of dot product attention, namely direct interaction between any two elements in a sequence (up to proper masking).
comments.explanation: AFT does provide a global receptive field: every output position can depend on information aggregated from every allowed key/value position. However, standard dot-product attention also has query-dependent pairwise scoring, such as q_t^T k_t′. In AFT, the key/value sequence is pooled with weights that do not depend on the particular target query, and the query only gates the pooled vector elementwise. Thus AFT does not preserve the full query-conditioned token-token comparison structure of attention. The claim should be qualified as preserving global access/connectivity rather than the full pairwise adaptivity of dot-product attention.

comments.title: Softmax-key causal decoding requires an additional cached denominator
comments.quote: Also note that the same space and time complexity still holds for _σk_ = _softmax_ , both in training and decoding.
comments.explanation: The recurrence given for causal decoding is directly valid when σq and σk are element-wise functions, because one can cache a running sum of σk(K′) ⊙ V′. With σk = softmax over the context dimension, however, σk is not an element-wise function of a single token because the normalization depends on the whole prefix. The same asymptotic O(d) decoding cost is still possible by caching both a numerator sum, ∑ exp(K′_t′) ⊙ V′_t′, and a denominator sum, ∑ exp(K′_t′), but this extra cached state is not spelled out. The asymptotic claim is likely correct, but the written recurrence is incomplete for the softmax-key variant.

comments.title: AFT-local-learned parameterization and window semantics are underspecified
comments.quote: Note that in this case, we maintain dense connection between every _t, t[′]_ pair, but rather introduce learnable biases for more recent contexts. ... The learned version also adds very little overhead to both the time and space complexity, as _w_ is sparse and static, whose memory cost is marginalized out across batches during training.
comments.explanation: The local learned variant is described in ways that are difficult to reconcile. The text says it maintains dense connections between every pair, but also says w is sparse and static; elsewhere it is summarized as using a mask with only a few learnable entries, while the earlier definition appears to induce a dense pairwise positional bias through a low-dimensional parameterization. More importantly, a target-dependent scalar w_{t,t′} can break the cumulative-sum recurrence that gives causal AFT its O(Td) training and O(d) decoding behavior, unless the sparse/local structure is implemented in a specific efficient way. The meaning of the local window size is also unclear for edge cases such as s = 0, since the variant retains global connections outside the learned local bias. The paper should clarify exactly what part of w is sparse or dense, how the learned local terms are computed efficiently, and what each window-size setting means.

comments.title: Decoding benchmark is described as the same setup, but Figure 3 uses a different feature dimension
comments.quote: For all of these, we used a base architecture with 12 layers, 8 heads (except for AFT, where there are no heads), feature dimension of either 256 or 1024, and context lengths up to 10,000.
comments.explanation: The efficiency subsection appears to define one benchmark setup for both the training-time forward/backward comparison and the decoding comparison, and later says decoding speed is examined “in the same benchmark.” However, Figure 3 is labeled “dim = 64,” whereas the described setup says the feature dimension is either 256 or 1024. This may simply mean the same random-data protocol was used with a different dimensionality for decoding, but the hyperparameters for the decoding benchmark are not clearly specified. Since runtime comparisons are sensitive to feature dimension, this inconsistency should be clarified.

comments.title: Figure 4 caption says three variants while the figure and text describe four
comments.quote: Figure 4: Proof of concept experiments for AFT-relu2, AFT-relu and AFT-softmax, tested on CIFAR10. All three versions train well with standard optimization settings. AFT-relu2 and AFT-relu perform similarly, while AFT-relu-softmax and AFT-sigmoid-softmax are more stable and yields significantly better results.
comments.explanation: The surrounding text says four nonlinearity variants are studied, and the Figure 4 legend lists AFT-relu, AFT-relu2, AFT-relu-softmax, and AFT-sigmoid-softmax. The caption, however, first refers to three versions and names only AFT-relu2, AFT-relu, and AFT-softmax, before later distinguishing two softmax variants. This appears to be a caption-editing error, but it can confuse which variants were actually evaluated.

comments.title: Machine translation comparison is underqualified and insufficiently specified
comments.quote: In our experiments, we replace the multi-headed decoder self-attention blocks. We compare perplexity (PPL), BLEU score, and efficiency between the Transformer base and AFT in Table 5. In this task, we see that AFT performs on par with the Transformer.
comments.explanation: The paper states that only the decoder self-attention blocks are replaced, meaning the encoder self-attention and encoder-decoder cross-attention apparently remain standard attention. Therefore Table 5 supports the narrower claim that replacing decoder causal self-attention with AFT does not hurt this WMT14 En-De setup, not that a fully AFT-based encoder-decoder architecture performs on par with a Transformer. In addition, the WMT14 setup lacks details needed to reproduce or interpret the comparison, such as tokenization/BPE, BLEU variant, optimizer and learning-rate schedule, label smoothing, batching, checkpoint averaging, beam size, length penalty, and exact OpenNMT baseline configuration. The experiment should be described and framed more precisely.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper presents a potentially interesting and timely simplification of Transformer attention, with a clear motivation around reducing the quadratic cost of dot-product attention and an appealingly simple proposed mechanism with linear training complexity and constant-time autoregressive decoding. The high-level exposition is generally understandable, and the claimed empirical breadth—image modeling, language modeling, translation, and point clouds—suggests the authors aim to establish AFT as a serious drop-in alternative rather than a narrow efficiency trick. However, the central conceptual claims need much more careful qualification: the assertion that AFT “maintains the full advantage” of attention via direct interaction between all sequence elements seems overstated, since the described key-value pooling appears to collapse token-specific pairwise interactions into a global or cumulative summary modulated elementwise by the query, which is likely less expressive than standard attention unless positional/local biases or other mechanisms compensate. The novelty should also be more clearly situated relative to linear attention, pooling-based sequence models, gated global-context modules, and related efficient Transformer variants; simply not approximating softmax attention does not by itself establish a fundamentally new design space. The complexity discussion is attractive but should include practical wall-clock/memory measurements, constant factors, implementation details, and decoding-cache assumptions, especially for fair comparison with optimized attention variants. Clarity is weakened by notation and formatting issues in the excerpt, including missing equations, minor typos, ambiguous broadcasting conventions, and an underdeveloped explanation of the claimed relationship between AFT and extreme multi-head attention. Overall, the paper has a promising and elegant idea, but its most significant risks are overclaiming expressivity and “attention-free” advantages, insufficient theoretical characterization of what dependencies AFT can or cannot model, and the need for especially rigorous empirical comparisons and ablations to show that performance gains are due to the proposed mechanism rather than architectural or tuning differences.

comments.title: Asymptotic complexity appears to exclude the required dense Q/K/V projections
comments.quote: During training time, AFT has linear time and space complexity w.r.t. both the sequence length and feature dimension; in the autoregressive decoding mode, AFT has constant memory and time complexity per step.
comments.explanation: I initially read this as referring to the AFT operation that replaces the attention matrix computation, and in that restricted sense the later AFT pooling/elements-wise operation can indeed be O(Td) in training and O(d) per decoding step. However, the methodology immediately defines AFT as first computing Q′ = QW[Q], K′ = KW[K], V′ = V W[V]. With dense d-by-d projections, those transformations cost O(Td^2) during training and O(d^2) per autoregressive step. Standard Transformer analyses sometimes omit the common Q/K/V projection costs when comparing attention mechanisms, so the authors’ intent is understandable. But the text states the complexity of “AFT” and “training time” rather than explicitly saying “excluding the shared linear projections” or “for the attention-free mixing operation only.” As written, the headline claim of linear complexity in the feature dimension and O(d) decoding time is not true for the full layer they define.

comments.title: Claim that AFT preserves the full pairwise-interaction advantage of attention is overstated
comments.quote: AFT maintains the full advantage of dot product attention, namely direct interaction between any two elements in a sequence (up to proper masking).
comments.explanation: The authors are right that, unlike a fixed-window convolution or a purely recurrent update, vanilla AFT allows every output position to depend on information aggregated from every key/value position, and the dependency path is short. That partially resolves the concern if “direct interaction” is meant only as global receptive field. However, in standard dot-product attention the interaction between positions t and t′ is query-dependent through a pair-specific score such as q_t^T k_t′. In AFT, the key/value sequence is first pooled into a reduced representation whose weights do not depend on the particular query position, and the query only gates the already-pooled vector elementwise. Thus AFT does not preserve the full pairwise, query-conditioned token-token comparison that is usually considered a core advantage of attention. The statement is therefore a misleading overclaim unless qualified as preserving global access/receptive field rather than the full interaction structure.

comments.title: Complexity claim appears to exclude the dense Q/K/V projections
comments.quote: The resulting operation has an extremely efficiency of _O_ ( _Td_ ) w.r.t. both time and space, which is the first model that achieves **linear complexity along both context and feature dimensions** . Moreover, the causal mode of AFT has an additional advantage of a constant decoding cost per step, similar to (Katharopoulos et al., 2020).
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ intended comparison: the attention-mixing part of AFT, after Q/K/V have already been projected, uses only element-wise operations and pooling, so that sub-operation is indeed O(Td), and the causal update can be O(d) per step with cached state. However, just before this passage AFT is defined as first applying dense linear projections Q′ = QW^Q, K′ = KW^K, V′ = VW^V. Those projections cost O(Td^2) during training and O(d^2) per decoding step if counted as part of the AFT layer. Standard attention-complexity tables often omit the shared projection cost, but the wording here says the model/layer achieves linear complexity in both context and feature dimensions and constant O(d) decoding cost, which a careful reader could reasonably interpret as including the projections just introduced. This should be qualified explicitly as the complexity of the AFT mixing operation excluding dense input/output projections; otherwise the stated feature-dimension scaling is not literally correct.

comments.title: Softmax-key causal decoding requires an additional cached denominator
comments.quote: Also note that the same space and time complexity still holds for _σk_ = _softmax_ , both in training and decoding.
comments.explanation: The earlier recurrence is correct under the stated assumption that σq and σk are element-wise functions: one can cache KV_t = ∑_{t′≤t} σk(K′_{t′}) ⊙ V′_{t′}. I then checked the softmax variant, where σk is normalized along the context dimension; in that case σk is not an element-wise function of a single K′_{t′}, because the normalization depends on the whole prefix. The O(d) decoding claim still seems salvageable: one can cache both the numerator ∑ exp(K′_{t′}) ⊙ V′_{t′} and the denominator ∑ exp(K′_{t′}), each a d-dimensional vector. What remains problematic is that the text moves from the element-wise recurrence to the softmax case without spelling out this extra cached state. The asymptotic claim is right, but the decoding recursion as written is not directly applicable to the default sigmoid+softmax setting.

comments.title: “Direct interaction” may overstate the pairwise adaptivity of AFT
comments.quote: Concretely, AFT enables _direct_ interaction of any two elements within the sequence, which is arguably the biggest advantage of Transformers over other types of models such as RNNs and ConvNets.
comments.explanation: The authors are right that in vanilla/causal AFT every past value can contribute to every later output, so there is a direct computational path from any source position to any target position up to masking. However, in standard Transformer terminology, the key advantage of attention is often not only all-to-all connectivity but content-dependent pairwise query-key scoring. In AFT, the pooled value representation is weighted by keys only, and the target query gates the pooled representation featurewise; there is no pair-specific compatibility score between Q_t and K_{t′}. The following sentence partially acknowledges this by saying the weights depend only on the keys, so the issue is mostly one of framing. Still, saying AFT keeps the “full advantage” of direct interaction could mislead readers into thinking it preserves the same pairwise adaptive interactions as dot-product attention.

comments.title: Learned local AFT weights are described as both dense and sparse, leaving the efficient implementation unclear
comments.quote: Note that in this case, we maintain dense connection between every _t, t[′]_ pair, but rather introduce learnable biases for more recent contexts. We typically set _du_ to be a small number (e.g., 64) which greatly reduces the amount of additional parameters compared to learning a full matrix. The learned version also adds very little overhead to both the time and space complexity, as _w_ is sparse and static, whose memory cost is marginalized out across batches during training.
comments.explanation: I can see the intended design: use a position-based local bias so nearby positions receive learnable corrections while still allowing nonzero contribution from the rest of the prefix. That could plausibly be implemented efficiently if the out-of-window weights have a simple shared form and only local corrections must be materialized. But as written, the description first says the model maintains dense connections between every pair (t,t′), then justifies the overhead by saying w is sparse and static. Those statements are not obviously compatible. More importantly, a target-dependent scalar w_{t,t′} generally destroys the simple cumulative-sum recurrence that gives causal AFT its O(Td) training and O(d) decoding behavior; computing arbitrary dense w-weighted sums would be O(T^2d). The text does not sufficiently specify what part of w is sparse, how the dense out-of-window contributions are accumulated, or why the low-rank/local parameterization preserves the claimed efficiency. This is likely fixable with an implementation detail, but it is currently underspecified for reproducing the local learned variant and verifying its complexity.

comments.title: Decoding benchmark is described as the same setup, but Figure 3 uses a different feature dimension
comments.quote: For all of these, we used a base architecture with 12 layers, 8 heads (except for AFT, where there are no heads), feature dimension of either 256 or 1024, and context lengths up to 10,000.
comments.explanation: I initially read the efficiency subsection as defining one benchmark setup for both the training-time forward/backward comparison and the decoding comparison. That interpretation is reinforced a few sentences later by the statement that decoding speed is examined “in the same benchmark.” However, Figure 3 is labeled “dim = 64,” whereas the described setup here says the feature dimension is either 256 or 1024. This may simply mean that the authors used the same random-data benchmarking protocol but a different dimensionality for decoding, perhaps to make long autoregressive decoding runs practical. Still, as written, the hyperparameters for the decoding benchmark are not clearly specified and appear inconsistent with the stated setup. Since runtime comparisons are sensitive to feature dimension—especially for linear attention, whose decoding cost scales roughly as \(d^2\)—this should be clarified.

comments.title: AFT-local-learned is characterized as having only a few learnable entries
comments.quote: AFT can be viewed as a more drastic version in this direction, where the we adopt a single global ”attention mask” ( _w_ ) of all ones (vanilla AFT) or with a few learnable entries (AFT-local-learned).
comments.explanation: I understand the intended contrast: vanilla AFT uses no pairwise learned attention matrix, and AFT-local-learned introduces a lightweight learned positional bias rather than full dot-product attention. However, from the earlier definition, AFT-local-learned appears to parameterize a dense pairwise locality bias through low-dimensional position embeddings \(u,v \in \mathbb{R}^{T \times d_u}\), not literally a mask with only a few learnable entries. The number of parameters is reduced relative to a full \(T \times T\) matrix, but the induced \(w_{t,t'}\) can still be dense over pairs. A careful reader could therefore misunderstand the learned-local variant as using a sparse mask with a small number of trainable pairwise weights, rather than a low-rank/dense positional bias. Rephrasing to “a low-dimensional learned positional bias” or “a few learnable parameters per position” would better match the method.

comments.title: Figure 4 caption says three variants while the figure and text describe four
comments.quote: Figure 4: Proof of concept experiments for AFT-relu2, AFT-relu and AFT-softmax, tested on CIFAR10. All three versions train well with standard optimization settings. AFT-relu2 and AFT-relu perform similarly, while AFT-relu-softmax and AFT-sigmoid-softmax are more stable and yields significantly better results.
comments.explanation: The surrounding text says the authors study four nonlinearity variants, and the legend in Figure 4 lists AFT-relu, AFT-relu2, AFT-relu-softmax, and AFT-sigmoid-softmax, each with train/test curves. The caption’s first two sentences, however, refer to “AFT-relu2, AFT-relu and AFT-softmax” and then “All three versions,” while the final sentence distinguishes two different softmax variants. I considered whether “AFT-softmax” was intended as an umbrella name for both softmax-key variants, but the final sentence and legend make the four-way distinction operationally important. This is likely just a caption-editing error, but it can confuse which variants were actually evaluated.

comments.title: AFT translation result is a hybrid model, so the comparison is easy to overread
comments.quote: In our experiments, we replace the multi-headed decoder self-attention blocks. We compare perplexity (PPL), BLEU score, and efficiency between the Transformer base and AFT in Table 5. In this task, we see that AFT performs on par with the Transformer.
comments.explanation: The authors do state the crucial experimental choice: only the decoder self-attention blocks are replaced. That resolves an initial possible misunderstanding that the full encoder-decoder Transformer has been converted to AFT. However, the subsequent shorthand comparison between “Transformer base and AFT” and the conclusion that “AFT performs on par with the Transformer” remain somewhat misleading. In a standard encoder-decoder translation model, a large fraction of the computation and modeling capacity remains in the non-causal encoder self-attention and the encoder-decoder cross-attention, both of which appear to still use ordinary multi-head attention. Thus Table 5 supports the narrower claim that replacing decoder causal self-attention with AFT does not hurt WMT14 En-De performance in this setup; it does not by itself establish that an AFT-based encoder-decoder architecture performs on par with a Transformer. This is especially relevant because the paper elsewhere emphasizes AFT as a drop-in replacement for MHA, while this experiment replaces only one of the attention submodules.

comments.title: Machine translation setup lacks enough detail for a reproducible WMT14 comparison
comments.quote: As a machine translation benchmark, we show experiments with the WMT 2014 English to German translation task. The training set contains approximately 4.5 million sentence pairs. We compare against a Transformer architecture baseline using the OpenNMT implementation (Klein et al., 2017).
comments.explanation: The high-level benchmark description is standard, and using OpenNMT gives some context for the implementation. Still, for WMT14 En-De, the reported BLEU and perplexity are quite sensitive to details that are not specified here: tokenization and BPE/vocabulary construction, whether BLEU is tokenized or detokenized/SacreBLEU, model hyperparameters, batching schedule, optimizer and learning-rate schedule, label smoothing, checkpoint averaging, decoding beam size and length penalty, and whether the Transformer baseline is exactly OpenNMT’s base configuration or modified to match AFT. Because Table 5 is used to support a performance and efficiency comparison, these omitted choices make the result difficult to reproduce or interpret against standard WMT14 conventions. This may be specified elsewhere in an appendix, but within the provided context it remains underspecified.

comments.title: Meaning of window size 0 for AFT-local-learned is not clearly defined
comments.quote:
fixing everything but varying the local window size _s_ .

Table 4: Training and testing bpc w.r.t. the local window size for AFT-local-learned.

|Window size|0
1
2
4
8
32
64
128
256
512
1024|
comments.explanation: The motivation for sweeping the local window size is clear, and the table consistently shows a best value at 32. The remaining ambiguity is specific to AFT-local-learned: earlier, this variant is described as using locality as a learned bias rather than as a hard constraint, with nonzero connections retained outside the window. In that setting, a “window size” is not simply the number of attended tokens, so the semantics of very small values—especially `s = 0`—need to be explicit. Does `s = 0` mean no learned local bias, a purely global/full AFT baseline, exclusion of the current token, or some implementation-specific boundary case? Similarly, whether the current position is counted in `s = 1` matters for causal modeling. A careful reader can infer that the authors probably intend `s` to control how many recent positions receive learnable local bias, but the table includes edge cases whose exact implementation is not recoverable from the description.
